title: Discontent
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ LAURENCE RABY.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Laurence:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I said to young Allan Mן¿½Ilveray,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Beside the swift swirls of the North,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When, in lilac shot through with a silver ray,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We haulן¿½d the strong salmon fish forth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Said only, ן¿½He gave us some trouble
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To land him, and what does he weigh?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our friend has caught one that weighs double,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The game for the candle wonן¿½t pay
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Us to-day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We may tie up our rods and away.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I said to old Norman Mן¿½Gregor,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Three leagues to the west of Glen Dhu,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I had drawn, with a touch of the trigger,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The best bead that ever I drew,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Said merely, ן¿½For birds in the stubble
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I once had an eye, I could swear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½s down, but heן¿½s not worth the trouble
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of seeking. You once shot a bear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In his lair,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis only a buck that lies there.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I said to Lord Charles only last year,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The time that we toppן¿½d the oak rail
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Between Whartonן¿½s plough and Whynneן¿½s pasture,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And clearן¿½d the big brook in Blakesvale,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We only, at Warburtonן¿½s double
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He fell, then I finishן¿½d the run
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And killן¿½d clean, said, ן¿½So bursts a bubble
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That shone half an hour in the sun,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What is won?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your sire clearן¿½d and captured a gun.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I said to myself, in true sorrow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I said yestereן¿½en, ן¿½A fair prize
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is won, and it may be to-morrow
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twill not seem so fair in thine eyes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Real life is a race through sore trouble,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That gains not an inch on the goal,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bliss an intangible bubble
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That cheats an unsatisfied soul,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the whole
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the rest an illegible scroll.ן¿½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Adam Lindsay Gordon represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Adam Lindsay Gordon's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
